title: Strain engineering of Andreev spin qubits in Germanium
authors: Vittorio Coppini, Patrick Del Vecchio, Antonio L. R. Manesco, Anton Akhmerov, Valla Fatemi, Bernard van Heck, Stefano Bosco
abstract: Planar germanium heterostructures are promising hosts for hybrid quantum devices due to their compatibility with superconductors, low material disorder, and relaxed fabrication constraints. Also, the potentially low density of nuclear spins and strong spin-orbit interaction make germanium attractive for coherent spin physics. However, recent microwave spectroscopy experiments were unable to resolve a spin-splitting of bound states in germanium Josephson junctions, the prerequisite for defining and controlling Andreev spin qubits. Here, we argue that compressive strain is the key mechanism suppressing spin splitting in current devices. Furthermore, we propose unstrained and tensile-strained heterostructures, fully compatible with state-of-the-art growth technology, that significantly enhance the relevant spin-orbit effect. By numerically simulating ballistic Josephson junctions, we predict spin splittings comfortably in the GHz range, more than 2 orders of magnitude larger than compressively strained cases, and all-electric quantum gates in a hundred nanoseconds. Our results establish strain engineering as a key design principle for realizing Andreev spin qubits in germanium-based devices.
summary: This paper addresses the difficulty of implementing Andreev spin qubits in germanium due to suppressed spin-splitting caused by compressive strain. By proposing alternative heterostructures, such as unstrained Ge or tensile-strained GeSn, the authors demonstrate that spin-splitting can be enhanced by two orders of magnitude into the GHz range. The study also predicts that these systems allow for fast, all-electric quantum gate operations in approximately 100 nanoseconds. Ultimately, the work establishes strain engineering as a vital design principle for future germanium-based quantum computing architectures.
main_problem: Recent experiments failed to resolve the spin-splitting of Andreev bound states in germanium Josephson junctions, which is a prerequisite for controlling Andreev spin qubits.
main_result: The authors identify compressive strain as the cause of suppressed spin-splitting and propose that using unstrained or tensile-strained (GeSn) heterostructures can increase spin-splitting by over two orders of magnitude, enabling fast all-electric quantum gates.
method: The study uses a 6-band Luttinger-Kohn and Bir-Pikus Hamiltonian reduced to an effective 14x14 and 2x2 model via Schrieffer-Wolff transformations, combined with numerical simulations of ballistic Josephson junctions using the Kwant software package.
model_or_system: The system consists of planar germanium (Ge) heterostructure Josephson junctions, specifically comparing compressively strained Ge/SiGe, unstrained Ge, and tensile-strained Ge/GeSn architectures.
key_observables: Andreev level energy splitting (delta E), Fermi velocity difference (delta v), Rabi frequency (Omega_01), and coherence length (xi).
important_parameters: Strain levels (Si or Sn concentration), junction geometry (length L_N and width W), chemical potential (mu), and superconducting phase difference (phi).
main_assumptions: Assumes homogeneous strain from lattice mismatch, s-wave superconductivity induced from aluminum leads, and ballistic transport in the junctions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows junction geometry and how strain affects band dispersion and Andreev levels; Figure 2 shows dependence of Andreev levels on chemical potential and geometry; Figure 3 illustrates spin-splitting in tensile-strained Ge/GeSn; Figure 4 shows Rabi frequency for the qubit transition.
paper_structure: The paper begins by identifying the problem of suppressed spin-splitting, introduces the strain-dependent Hamiltonian and effective models, presents numerical simulations of different strain regimes (compressive, unstrained, tensile), evaluates qubit control via electric-dipole-spin-resonance, and concludes with design principles for Andreev spin qubits.
why_interesting: This paper is relevant for researchers in quantum information as it provides a concrete material engineering strategy (strain engineering) to realize a new class of controllable spin qubits (Andreev spin qubits) using semiconductor heterostructures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. ASQs in Ge. (a) Ge Josephson junction of length LN. Two aluminum leads with gap ∆, phase-difference ϕ, and width W, are coupled to a Ge heterostructure compris- ing a 10 nm Si0.2Ge0.8 barrier, a 15 nm compressive-strained ε-Ge (tensile-strained ¯ε-Ge) channel, and a bottom relaxed barrier of SixGe1−x (Ge1−ySny). At x = y = 0, the Ge channel is unstrained and accumulated by the electric field Ez = 1 mV/nm. (b) Band dispersion (upper panel) of ε-Ge at x = 2% (black) and x = 10% (blue). As x (i.e., compressive strain) increases, the SOI-induced difference of Fermi veloci- ties δv of the two spins substantially decreases (lower panel). (c) ϕ-dependent energy of Andreev levels in...

Figure 2. Andreev spin splitting in ε-Ge. (a) Coherence lengths ξ (upper panel) and energy levels (lower panel) against µ in an unstrained Ge junction. We show ξ of different spins (solid lines), and their average (dashed lines) for different sub-bands. The dots mark the values of the ξ used in (b) and (c); the arrow marks the value of µ in Figs. 1(c)-(d). (b) The spin-splitting ∆E rapidly decreases from the GHz range for unstrained Ge as the Si concentration x in the barrier, which determines the compressive strain of Ge, increases. Solid and dashed lines correspond to numerical simulations and Eq. (4), respectively. In panels (a)-(b) we use LN = 300 nm, W = 100 nm, ϕ = π/2. (c) Dependence...

Figure 3. Andreev spin splitting in ¯ε-Ge (a) Band dispersion of ¯ε-Ge at Sn concentration y = 10%, see Fig. 1, and (b) cor- responding Fermi-velocity difference of the spins of the LH ground-state. (c) Spin-splitting ∆E in a ¯ε-Ge junction ob- tained from Eq. (4) against y for different value of ξ. The advantage of ¯ε-Ge is apparent by comparing the black lines representing the maximum ∆E in the allowed range of µ be- tween the bottom of the first and second sub-band for ¯ε-Ge (solid line) and unstrained Ge (dash-dotted line). We use LN = 300 nm, W = 50 nm, ϕ = π/2.

Figure 4. Driving ASQs. Rabi frequency Ω01 for the intra- doublet transition |0⟩→|1⟩against ϕ for different values of ξ. We consider electric-dipole-spin-resonance driving generated by a resonant AC potential with amplitude δV = 100 µV in an unstrained Ge junction with LN = 300 nm and W = 100 nm. For ξ = 175 nm, this is the same system whose energy levels are shown in Fig. 1(c).

Figure 1. Energies Eτ j as a function of the concentration x and y. The zero of energy is pinned to the HH ground state, i.e. EH 1 ≡0. The shaded gray area marks the region excluded from the analysis in the main text to avoid the crossing between HH and LH ground states.

Figure 2. E(k∥) dispersion from the full Hamiltonian (black) computed from a 400-dimensional Hilbert space and from the effective Hamiltonian Heff (green). Top row: 2nd order SWT with A = {H1, H2, H3, H4, H5, η1, η2}. Bottom row: 1st order SWT with A = {H1, · · · , H20, η1, · · · , η10}. (a) Compressive strained Ge with x = 20%. (b) Unstrained Ge. (c) Tensile strained Ge with y = 2%. (d) Tensile strained Ge with y = 13%. Ez = 1 mV/nm.

Fig. 2 shows the energy dispersion of compressive strained Ge (x = 20%), relaxed Ge (x = 0%), slightly tensile strained Ge (y = 2%) and highly tensile strained Ge (y = 13%) computed from the diagonalization of the full k · p matrix HGe compared to the dispersion provided by the effective Hamiltonian Heff. The dimension of the full Hilbert space is 2 · 200 = 400.

Figure 3. (a) Left panel: −β1 (left y-axis, dashed line) and β3 (right y-axis, solid line) as a function of the Sn content y. Right panel: β2 as a function of the Si content x. The right y-axis in both panels share the same units and scale. Ez = 1 mV/nm. (b) Maximum velocity difference against strain, for the two lowest bands of a germanium lead with W = 100 nm. The maximum is taken in the µ range where only the first sub-band is occupied.
Figure 4. Sketch of a germanium junction. Each dot is a site and each line represents an hopping term. The orange area is the normal region while the blue areas are the superconducting regions.
Figure 6. Phase dependence of the off-diagonal elements of ∂H/∂ϕ.
